metadata.title: Agent Battle: Minecraft as an AI Benchmark

beats.narration_text: Using Minecraft as an AI benchmark intrigues me because it's open-ended in a way chess never was. I want to understand what it actually measures. Help me: (1) name the capabilities a Minecraft agent battle tests that a static QA benchmark can't — long-horizon planning, tool use, resource management; (2) explain how you'd score two competing agents fairly when there's no single win condition; (3) design one task within the game that would expose whether an agent is truly planning ahead or just reacting greedily step to step.

props.command: Using Minecraft as an AI benchmark intrigues me because it's open-ended in a way chess never was. I want to understand what it actually measures. Help me: (1) name the capabilities a Minecraft agent battle tests that a static QA benchmark can't — long-horizon planning, tool use, resource management; (2) explain how you'd score two competing agents fairly when there's no single win condition; (3) design one task within the game that would expose whether an agent is truly planning ahead or just reacting greedily step to step.
output: Game: mine diamonds in 5 minutes, all agents same world
Score: most diamonds wins; fewest tokens breaks ties
Config knobs: model + system prompt + skills + MCP tools
Result: prompt engineering becomes a measurable sport

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The setup is surgical. Your bot connects outbound to Anthropic's event server — nothing exposed, no tunnels, just a key. The cloud agent drives a local Minecraft bot over MCP. Every participant starts at y=-40 with an iron pickaxe and the same materials. Anthropic's infrastructure handles the relay. You handle the prompt.

props.artifactHeading: Outbound connection. No ports exposed. One key.
artifactLines: Bot connects outbound → event relay → cloud agent
No tunnels created on your machine
Start position: y=-40, iron pickaxe, shared materials
minute cap enforced by leaderboard, not your code
Best single run counts — unlimited attempts

beats.narration_text: The four knobs you tune: model, system prompt, skills, and MCP tools. That's it. The default agent with an empty system prompt still gets diamonds — Claude knows Minecraft. The competition is efficiency. The default chats too much, polls state redundantly, doesn't relocate after a vein runs dry. A tuned system prompt fixes all three.

props.artifactHeading: model · system prompt · skills · MCP tools
artifactLines: model — which Claude variant runs your agent
system — your entire strategic advantage
SKILLS=[SKILL_MINING] — opt-in mining subroutine
EXTRA_MCP — attach wiki lookup, inventory tools
ALLOWED_TOOLS — scope what the agent can call

beats.narration_text: The tiebreaker is tokens — and that's the real lesson. An AI-written bloated prompt often loses to three targeted lines a human wrote themselves. Attaching the wiki MCP without a prompt nudge gives the agent a lookup tool it never calls. A prompt that ignores the start kit sends the agent crafting things it already has. Both failures are real lessons about agent composition.

props.artifactHeading: Fewer tokens wins ties. Bloat is a penalty.
artifactLines: Tiebreaker: fewest tokens across the 5-minute run
AI-generated verbose prompts often lose to 3 focused lines
MCP tool attached but never called = wasted context
Prompt ignoring start_kit → crafting already-owned items
Fast feedback: --eval runs decision probes in 30-60 seconds

beats.narration_text: The deeper point: Minecraft is a proxy for any real agent task. You have a goal, a time budget, a tool set, and a token budget. The workshop makes all four constraints explicit and scoreable. That's what most agent evals don't do. When you can run your config on decision probes in 30 seconds and see exactly which calls it gets wrong, prompt engineering stops being art and starts being engineering.

props.artifactHeading: Four constraints. All explicit. All scoreable.
artifactLines: Goal: diamonds (unambiguous, single metric)
Time budget: 5-minute hard cap, enforced externally
Tool set: pickaxe, skills, MCP — scoped and auditable
Token budget: tracked, tiebreaker, first-class constraint
Eval loop: --eval scores decision probes in under 60 seconds

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Ask Claude: 'I'm building an agent that needs to complete a task efficiently in under 5 minutes with a strict token budget. Write me a system prompt that prioritizes decisive action over verification loops, relocates after each resource is depleted, and never crafts items it already has.' That prompt will reveal whether Claude defaults to cautious over-checking or tight execution. The answer tells you more about agent design than any blog post. Liam, in for Bear.

props.command: I'm building an agent that needs to complete a task efficiently in under 5 minutes with a strict token budget. Write me a system prompt that prioritizes decisive action over verification loops, relocates after each resource is depleted, and never crafts items it already has.
props.segment: Agent Battle: Minecraft as an AI Benchmark
output: Claude drafts a compact, decisive system prompt
Reveals token-efficiency tradeoffs in agent design
Shows how constraint language shapes agent behavior
